metrics.narrative: Two quantified pain points surfaced on 2026-06-04 call. (1) Pay-on-time rate currently ~75%, target improvement to ~95% as a demonstrable P2P/AI outcome. Ming stated this would be 'very convincing to our CPO... we try now 3 years use different things, and we are still at 75%... if we can improve from 75% to 95%, that's a good solution'. (2) Supplier onboarding cycle currently 20-30 days due to multi-gate approvals (IT, finance, cybersecurity, supplier-quality—6-7 gates total), expectation is ~1 week for project needs. Ming confirmed 'it took us normally 20-30 days... we want a supplier, we want them maybe a week is reasonable. But if you take 3, 4 weeks, then it's no good' (2026-06-04). Both metrics are operational-excellence / P2P domain pain; no quantified sourcing/contracting savings articulated. CPO-level business case (cost reduction, procurement efficiency ROI) has not been developed. Gap: no quantified total addressable spend, headcount reduction target, or AI adoption ROI model presented to executive sponsors.

champion.narrative: Ming Li (Proposal Procurement Leader, North America strategic sourcing lead, Toronto/Kitchener-based) is the identified champion. Ming initiated contact, attended the 2026-05-12 intro call and the 2026-05-13 Agent AI Procurement Summit, organized the 2026-06-04 discovery call and the 2026-06-17 demo with ~10+ North America and European stakeholders, created an internal 'AI investor group' chat to broadcast Zycus materials, and presented an AI adoption strategy to the CPO ~two weeks before 2026-06-22. Ming stated 'I did make no recommendation and I'm still kind of looking for the feedback' (2026-06-22), showing advocacy. Ming has strong operational reach (multi-threaded the demo across regions, engaged P2P directors Fernando and Omar, category management, operational-excellence team) but weak executive/commercial influence. Ming explicitly stated 'I'm not involve other all other technology stuff... they are the backend people... they will be more interest and follow up based on their own tool budget' (2026-06-22), meaning Ming cannot drive budget approval or vendor selection—that authority sits with the operational-excellence/digital team (Josh) and the CPO. Ming has no EB access, no pricing authority, no ability to override the integration-first mandate. Champion strength: operationally ACTIVE (organizing calls, distributing content, advocating internally) but commercially WEAK (no budget authority, no EB access, siloed from decision-makers). At-risk factors: (1) Ming is going to Asia for two months starting late June 2026, reducing engagement cadence. (2) Ming's AI adoption strategy got no CPO response, signaling low executive sponsorship. (3) If the integration drags or Ariba's pilot succeeds, Ming's advocacy may fade as priorities shift. Current status: developing, at risk of cooling during the 1-2 year integration freeze unless Josh and the operational-excellence team convert into co-champions with budget influence.

Salesforce Competitor field: SAP Ariba
competition.narrative: Three competitive forces. (1) SAP Ariba (incumbent, active): Veolia uses Ariba for payment and limited RFQ/NDA functionality; Ariba adoption is partial but the operational-excellence team is testing Ariba's AI/RFQ automation capabilities in parallel as the default path before approving a new vendor. Ming stated 'since we had contract with Ariba for other things already, so they are trying to see what Ariba can come out with first... they are kind of challenging iRequest and asking them to provide us user cases and to pilot a few things, for example the automation of the RFQ stuff' (2026-06-22). So far 'haven't seen any user case success' (Ming, 2026-06-22), but if Ariba delivers a passable pilot during the 1-2 year integration freeze, they may block Zycus via inertia (good-enough incumbent, no procurement process required). Threat level: medium (weak current capability but entrenched relationship and testing window). (2) Do Nothing / Manual Status Quo (highest current threat): The Veolia-Suez integration freeze means ALL new tool approvals are blocked until 2027-2028. Ming confirmed 'they didn't approve any advancement budget... only for the integration... we had to wait until net happen, and then after net, we might be able to try to push your AI stuff into the agenda' (2026-06-22). The buyer is choosing integration completion over AI/procurement advancement, making 'do nothing new until integration is done' the de facto competitor. Threat level: high (active, buyer-imposed, 1-2 year timeline). (3) Unknown future ERP vendor: Veolia (SAP) and Suez (different ERP, possibly Great Plains per Ming 2026-06-22) are consolidating, and Ming stated 'I heard they are going to use a third one, neither net one, neither SAP' (2026-06-22). If the new ERP vendor bundles procurement modules or influences the tech stack decision, it could reshape the competitive landscape in 2027-2028. Threat level: dormant (unknown, 1-2 years out). How we win: demonstrate quantifiable P2P impact (75% → 95% pay-on-time) in a pilot or proof-of-concept DURING the integration freeze (parallel path, not sequential), position Zycus as ERP-agnostic and integration-flexible so the ERP consolidation decision does not gate Zycus adoption, and convert Josh (operational-excellence lead) into a co-champion who can advocate for a pilot budget exception.

identify_pain.narrative: Three operational/financial pain points confirmed across calls. (1) P2P invoice/PO matching and pay-on-time: currently ~75% on-time payment, causing supplier dissatisfaction and manual expediting workload for buyers. Ming stated 'payment issue... if we are able for example right now we are pay on time 75%, if we can improve to 95%, something like that would be very convincing to our CPO' (2026-06-04). Pain owner: P2P operations (Fernando, Omar). (2) Supplier onboarding cycle: 20-30 days due to multi-gate approvals (IT, finance, cybersecurity, supplier-quality), blocking project timelines. Ming stated 'it took us normally 20-30 days to complete... we want a supplier maybe a week is reasonable. But if you take 3, 4 weeks, then it's no good' (2026-06-04). Pain owner: operational-excellence team (Josh) and P2P operations. (3) Manual sourcing event management: RFP/RFX creation, vendor quote evaluation, commercial recommendations all manual, missing AI efficiency opportunity. Ming stated 'only strategic sourcing, the pain point is everything is still manual... the evaluation on the vendor quotes, I have queues, they're all manual... not a pain point for people doing the day job, but for the management there's a pain point here, we should be more efficient' (2026-06-04). Pain owner: category management and strategic sourcing (Ming's team). Additional context: Veolia uses SAP ERP and Ariba for payment, but Ariba adoption is partial ('not to the full extent... mainly for the payment... RFQs, NDAs still in very early exploration stage', Ming 2026-06-04). Separate contract system (not Ariba CLM), data scattered across emails for iRequest, intake not centralized. These are confirmed pains but the urgency is LOW because the buyer has deprioritized all advancement initiatives until the Veolia-Suez integration completes (2027-2028).

decision_process.narrative: Buyer is running an informal, multi-regional discovery process, not a formal procurement. On 2026-06-04, Ming convened North America stakeholders (category management, P2P operations, AP) for the demo; European operational-excellence team (6-7 people, unnamed) joined uninvited, indicating broad awareness and interest. Ming stated 'I was only planning for the North American team to join, but eventually European team found out and say hey we want to join' (2026-06-22). Post-demo, Ming consulted the operational-excellence team on next steps and confirmed they are the supporting function that 'evaluate the tools and technologies' (2026-06-22). The approval chain runs: operational-excellence/digital team (Josh) recommends → CPO (Paris) and executive leadership approve budget → procurement executes. Ming confirmed on 2026-06-22 that the Veolia-Suez integration is the only approved 2026 initiative and gates all new tool approvals: 'they didn't approve any advancement budget... only for the integration... we had to wait until net happen, and then after net, we might be able to try to push your AI stuff into the agenda'. So the decision process is: (1) integration completes (1-2 years, 2027-2028), (2) ERP consolidation decision finalized (SAP vs other), (3) business case for AI/procurement tools submitted to CPO, (4) budget approved, (5) vendor selection, (6) contract. Currently stuck at step 1. In parallel, Ariba is testing AI/RFQ automation as the default pilot ('they are trying to see what Ariba can come out with first'), so Ariba may satisfy 'good enough' criteria and block Zycus via inertia. Gap: no formal timeline for integration completion, no scheduled next milestone with Zycus, no executive briefing or business-case review planned.

decision_criteria.narrative: Buyer articulated three evaluation priorities on 2026-06-04 and 2026-06-22 calls. (1) Integration flexibility: must work alongside existing SAP ERP without forcing ERP replacement, and ideally coexist with Ariba during transition. Ming asked 'do you think you can demonstrate how you work with a customer who is using SAP and how your system integrate with their system?' (2026-06-04). (2) P2P operational impact: improve pay-on-time rate from 75% to 95% and reduce supplier onboarding from 20-30 days to ~1 week—quantifiable, near-term wins that would convince the CPO. (3) AI automation breadth: agentic AI for sourcing event creation (RFP/RFX), autonomous negotiation (tail spend), and intake orchestration (Teams-like conversational interface). Ming stated 'whatever AI can do, then we should do it' and wanted to see 'how Zycus work... without integrating with other systems... end to end' (2026-06-04, 2026-06-22). Gap: no formal RFP/scorecard, no weighted criteria, no procurement committee decision framework documented. The 2026-06-17 demo was well-received ('impressed', per Ming 2026-06-22) but did not produce a written evaluation or next-gate milestone. Decision process is currently informal discovery, not structured vendor selection.

ai_fit_signal.summary: AI Hungry. Ming stated 'whatever AI can do, then we should do it' (2026-06-04), presented an AI adoption strategy to the CPO recommending scaling AI usage because 'right now it's very slow and there's no obligation for the team to use it and the adoption is very slow' (2026-06-22), and created an internal AI investor group to broadcast AI tools and best practices. Buyer explicitly interested in agentic AI automation (sourcing events, autonomous negotiation, intake orchestration) and sees AI as a strategic procurement efficiency lever. Current AI adoption is low and optional; Ming's recommendation is to make it mandatory and scale it. Zycus AI capabilities (Merlin, agentic AI, autonomous negotiation) align perfectly with buyer appetite. Gap: appetite is at the operational/influencer level (Ming, Josh, P2P team); CPO-level AI mandate is absent (no response to Ming's strategy, no budget approved).

Last buyer call 2026-06-22 (4 days ago) was a rep-initiated check-in where Ming confirmed the deal is blocked on integration priority and budget freeze. north_star_verdict.math: Created 2026-05-20, qualified same day, 37 days old, 112 days to close 2026-10-16. Last buyer call 2026-06-22 (4 days ago, rep-initiated check-in). Stage Qualified with 112-day horizon requires validation, shortlist, commercials, EB sign-off, legal, and contracting in under 16 weeks—enterprise motion reality is 12-15 months minimum. Worse: buyer explicitly stated on 2026-06-22 that the Veolia-Suez acquisition integration is a 1-2 year priority blocking ANY advancement budget ('they didn't approve any advancement budget... only for the integration... we had to wait until net happen, and then after net, we might be able to try to push your AI stuff into the agenda'). Ming confirmed zero 2026 budget for new tools. Realistic timeline: integration completes 2027-2028, THEN business case, THEN procurement—actual close is 18-24 months out. Oct-26 date is a placeholder disconnected from buyer reality. Forward slip required: +18 months minimum.

north_star_verdict.headline: Integration freeze remains absolute. Buyer confirmed zero 2026 budget, 1-2 year wait mandated by Paris HQ until Veolia-Suez consolidation completes. No EB engagement, no commercial motion, no path to October close. Realistic timeline is 2027-2028.

flags: Pilot-first wedge against the integration freeze: The buyer's stated objection is 'we need to complete the Suez integration and decide the ERP platform first, THEN consider new tools' (1-2 year timeline). Challenge that sequencing with a pilot-first approach: a narrow, high-impact P2P pilot (on-time payment 75%→95%, supplier onboarding 20-30d→1wk) can run IN PARALLEL to the integration because Zycus is ERP-complementary (no SAP replacement required) and modular (deploy P2P independently, expand post-integration). Frame it as 'validate the AI ROI now on a contained use case so you can confidently scale it across the consolidated organization later, rather than wait 2 years and start from zero'. This is the ONLY path to pull the close date forward; otherwise, requalify to 2027-2028 pipeline. Position the pilot as a strategic hedge—if Ariba's automation pilots fail (as they have so far), Veolia needs a proven alternative ready to deploy when the integration completes.

Competitive displacement strategy vs Ariba: Ariba is the incumbent with low adoption and no successful AI pilots yet, but they have the operational-excellence team's attention by default ('let's see what Ariba can do first'). Our counter: build a direct comparison narrative—'Ariba is a point-solution bolt-on with limited AI depth; Zycus is the comprehensive, AI-native source-to-pay platform with proven P2P automation (share 2-3 customer proof points: on-time payment improvement, supplier onboarding acceleration, tail-spend savings via ANA). Ariba will incrementally patch; Zycus leapfrogs.' Share this narrative WITH Ming/Josh as a Leave-Behind they can use internally, and offer a bake-off pilot: 'Run a 90-day Zycus P2P pilot alongside Ariba's RFQ automation test; measure which delivers measurable operational improvement.' This forces a data-driven decision and neutralizes the incumbent advantage. If Ariba's pilots continue to underwhelm, we become the obvious choice when budget reopens.
best_practice_check.summary: Three critical win-strategy moves required now: (1) Negotiate a parallel pilot path during the integration freeze to preempt the 1-2 year wait and deliver quantifiable P2P impact before Ariba's pilot succeeds. (2) Convert Josh (operational-excellence lead) into a co-champion with budget influence; Ming alone cannot drive this deal. (3) Secure executive sponsorship (CPO or CFO briefing) to validate the business case and unblock budget—without EB engagement this deal will stall indefinitely.

competitive_position.summary: Ariba is the live peer threat but weak (partial adoption, no AI success yet); Do Nothing/integration-freeze is the strongest CURRENT threat blocking all progress for 1-2 years (buyer-mandated). Zycus wins by securing a parallel pilot exception during the freeze—demonstrate P2P impact (pay-on-time improvement) in a limited scope that does not require full platform deployment or ERP decision, converting Josh (operational-excellence) into a co-champion who can advocate for pilot budget. If we wait until integration completes (2027-2028), Ariba may satisfy good-enough criteria via inertia or the new ERP vendor may reshape the landscape. Act now on a pilot path or lose the window.

value.flag: No Economic Buyer engaged after 37 days and a well-received demo. CPO (Paris) received Ming's AI adoption strategy but gave no response, no budget approval, no mandate. The operational-excellence team (Josh) and P2P directors (Fernando, Omar) are influencers, not budget holders. Without EB access and executive sponsorship, this deal cannot progress to proposal or negotiation. Recommend: brief the deal owner's manager on the EB gap and request an executive connect (Zycus exec to the relevant stakeholder or CFO) to validate the business case, discuss the integration-freeze challenge, and explore pilot budget exception.

value.flag: Parallel pilot path required to overcome the 1-2 year integration freeze. Buyer has mandated that all new tool approvals wait until the Veolia-Suez integration completes (2027-2028) and has approved zero 2026 budget for Zycus. Waiting until 2027-2028 risks losing the deal to Ariba inertia (good-enough incumbent pilot) or a new ERP vendor's bundled modules. Recommend: position a limited-scope P2P proof-of-concept (e.g. pay-on-time improvement 75% → 95% in one North America region) that runs DURING the integration, requires minimal IT lift, delivers quantifiable results in 3-6 months, and does not gate or delay the integration. Frame it as an AI adoption learning initiative (aligned with the relevant stakeholder recommendation) that de-risks the full platform decision in 2027-2028. Engage Josh to champion this pilot path internally.
